channel.title: The Outer Worlds on Hi everyone. It's me, Tim.
channel.description: Recent content in The Outer Worlds on Hi everyone. It's me, Tim.

item.title: The Outer Worlds Launch Party Video
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to share with you something special: The Outer Worlds launch party video.

Let me explain so, when The Outer Worlds launched, we had a big launch party for it on October 25th 2019. It was awesome.

Um, most of obsidian was there, the development team, the publisher came in from uh private division and some of the The Outer Worlds — um, some of the Take 2 folks. We had a lot of other guests.

item.title: The Outer Worlds Vertical Slice
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I have a very special video to show you.

It is the The Outer Worlds’s vertical slice. Before I get in, let me give you some context, and actually, before I give you context, let me thank obsidian and especially Mikey Dowling for arranging this and letting me show this to you. I want to show you the vertical slice for many reasons, and let me let me explain what a vertical slice is first, if you watched my production stages video, which I link below, I talk about the different stages that a game goes through when it’s being developed. The vertical slice stage is when you make a level of the game or a map or, in this case, an outdoor map with a few connecting Maps. I think there was a town map and three dungeon Maps.

item.title: The Outer Worlds: Eight Playthroughs
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about eight playthroughs of The Outer Worlds.

Now I do have a video where I talk about play testing The Outer Worlds and I say I did it 16 times and I give a very high level overview of the playthroughs I did. I was digging through archives and I found much more detail on the first eight playthroughs. I did so, if this seems familiar, I’ll link the other video below, which is a very high level, but I’m going to actually read what I wrote for that also when I’m done. I found a list of purchase requests. It was a document I kept.

item.title: Marketing The Outer Worlds
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about marketing and specifically marketing on The Outer Worlds.

Now I’ve had a few other videos about The Outer Worlds and I know people want me to talk more about its development and the team and its design. But the thing is a lot of the things I would talk about are still ongoing with The Outer Worlds 2 and proprietary, so there’s not as much I talk about that game. So I’ve gone and talked about a lot of my other games. But then it occurred to me there was recently some news — you may have seen it — about the The Outer Worlds’s hitting 5 million sales. Now that doesn’t include game pass, because a lot of people on Xbox played it on Game Pass, so that’s not including people played it that way. This is people who bought — I think they call it — sell through, people who bought and have a copy of The Outer Worlds, whether it’s physical or digital, and that’s a lot. That’s. Um, I think it’s safe to say The Outer Worlds is my best selling game ever.

item.title: Playtesting The Outer Worlds
item.description:
Hi. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about play testing in the inner worlds.

The reason I want to focus on that is on some previous videos I talked about examples of bad play testing. That happened on my game, specifically Temple of Elemental Evil, where the external QA group decided they didn’t like playing bards, so they never played them, and we shipped with a really bad crash bug. Even though I played with a all-bard group, I never saw the bug, but they never played bards, so I was mad.

item.title: Branding in The Outer Worlds
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about corporate brands in the The Outer Worlds, which I think ties really nicely into yesterday’s video about coming up with names for Fallout.

So, in the The Outer Worlds, what you said — on a very distant colony ruled by a very dystopian group of Corporations. Now, we knew at the very early outset — you know, Leonard and I were working on the setting — we knew we were going to have a lot of Corporations because we knew we wanted that firefly-esque feeling to the setting and this required us to create a large number of companies and their slogans and their products and logos and slogans. When we did this, we had a plan in mind for how we wanted to do it and we also had a lot of influences that we knew we wanted to cover, um. There is one big origin for brands for me, but there’s a lot of influences on it too, so I kind of want to talk about that, the other things. That kind of factor in here is we want to talk how hard it is to humor, how Hobbies outside the game industry — which I’ve already talked about with the Disneyland one Disneyland video — how Hobbies you have will affect your game development. I also want to talk about getting the team all focused and going in One Direction, because there’s no way — I mean we weren’t going to do all of this. We were going to have other people on the team make companies and products. You know all the logos and everything had to be done.
